text: What if one simple change in your daily
coffee routine could help control your

your next sip, there's something
important you need to know that could
change your health. I'm not talking
about any miracle formula. And I'm not
selling anything. I'm talking about
coffee, the drink that's in your kitchen
right now, and one detail that changes
everything, the way and the timing in
which you drink that cup. Hi, I'm Dr.
Dr. Richard Ben, a physician. And in
recent years, I've closely followed
hundreds of patients with pre-diabetes
and type 2 diabetes, especially people
over 60. And what I'm going to tell you
today is something that has truly
changed many of their lives. Brazil is
facing a silent crisis. According to the
Ministry of Health itself, diabetes
cases have increased by 135% in less
than two decades in our country. Today,
nearly 20 million Brazilians have the
disease. And data from Vigotel, the
government's monitoring system, shows
that prevalence reaches an astonishing
30% among seniors over 65, one in every
three. It's much more than most people
imagine. And what worries me most in my
daily practice is not just those who
already have the diagnosis, but the huge
group of people who are right on the
edge with high glucose levels, with that
unexplained fatigue after lunch, with
that constant thirst that doesn't go
away, and who haven't yet been
diagnosed, or who have but don't know
what to do beyond taking medication and
waiting. Stay with me until the end of
this video because I'm going to show you
how a small adjustment, which costs
nothing, can make a huge difference in
controlling your glucose. But first, I
need to tell you something that most
people don't know about coffee. Imagine
your pancreas as a small factory that
produces insulin, the hormone
responsible for opening the doors of
your cells so that glucose can enter and
be turned into energy. When you eat,
glucose enters your bloodstream. This
factory goes into action, produces
insulin, and the glucose is absorbed by
the cells. Everything works fine. The
problem begins when this factory becomes
overloaded. When you eat a lot of foods
that spike your blood sugar quickly, the
pancreas has to work at its limit. Over
time, your cells become less sensitive
to insulin. The pancreas has to produce
more and more to achieve the same
effect, and that's when insulin
resistance sets in, the gateway to type
2 diabetes. Now, what about coffee? What
does it have to do with all this? Coffee
is an extraordinary drink from a
biochemical point of view. It contains
hundreds of bioactive compounds, but two
of them stand out to science.
Chlorogenic acid and trional. Think of
them as traffic controllers in your
intestines. When you drink coffee before
eating, these compounds make the
absorption of sugar from food slower and
more controlled. Instead of a surge of
glucose entering your bloodstream all at
once, you get a smooth flow that your
pancreas can manage without stress. A
study published in Diabetes Care
conducted by researchers from the
Harvard School of Public Health analyzed
data from more than 1 million people and
reached a conclusion that really
impressed me when I first read it.
Regular coffee consumption is associated
with a significant reduction in the risk
of developing type 2 diabetes. And it's
not a small effect. The risk decreases
in a dose- dependent way. The more cups
per day within a reasonable limit, the
lower the risk of developing the
disease. Then in 2018, researchers from
the Karolinska Institute in Sweden, one
of the most respected medical
institutions in the world, reviewed 30
different studies covering nearly 1.2 2
million participants and confirmed
coffee consumption, both regular and
decaffeinated, is associated with about
a 30% reduction in the risk of
developing type 2 diabetes.
But here's the point that almost no one
talks about. These benefits depend on
how you drink coffee because the wrong
coffee at the wrong time in the wrong
way can work against you. And that's
exactly what I see happening in my
practice far too often. Let me tell you
about Mr. Arnaldo, 72 years old,
retired, a man of habits. He had been
drinking coffee since he was 17 and was
proud of it. But when he came to my
office, his fasting glucose was 138 and
his HBA1C,
which reflects the average blood sugar
over the last 3 months, was 7.1%
above the desired level. He told me,
"Doctor, I do everything right. I drink
coffee every day. I walk three times a
week." So, I asked him to walk me
through his coffee routine in detail.
And that's when I discovered the
problem. Every day, as soon as he woke
up, before eating anything, he would
drink a large cup of strong black
coffee. Then, he would have another one
at 300 p.m. with a sweet biscuit and
sometimes a third before bed because he
thought it helped him relax. three
classic mistakes that I'm going to
explain to you now. Mistake number one,
drinking coffee on an empty stomach
right after waking up. This is the most
common one and the one that causes the
most harm. When you wake up after a
whole night of fasting, your body
already has elevated cortisol levels,
which is the stress hormone, the same
one your adrenal glands produce when
you're in an urgent situation. Cortisol
already signals your liver to release
glucose into the bloodstream so you have
energy to start the day. It's a normal
survival mechanism. The problem is that
caffeine amplifies this effect. It
further stimulates the adrenal glands,
releases more adrenaline, and the liver
responds by releasing even more glucose
into the blood. And you haven't eaten
anything yet. The result? You haven't
even taken a bite of food and your
glucose has already risen between 20 and
40 milligrams per deciliter. I've seen
lab results that confirm this in
patients who continuously monitor their
glucose. A study published in the
British Journal of Nutrition confirmed
this effect. Drinking black coffee on an
empty stomach after a night of sleep can
significantly increase blood glucose
levels in the morning. Mistake number
two, coffee with sugar, artificial
sweetener, or cream. It may seem
harmless, but it's not. A spoonful of
sugar in coffee causes an immediate
spike in glucose and cancels out the
benefits of the chlorogenic acid you
just consumed. It's like starting a fire
and pouring gasoline on it at the same
time. and artificial sweeteners. Many
people think they're the perfect
solution, but recent research, including
a study published in the scientific
journal Nutrition in 2021, suggests that
artificial sweeteners like aspartame and
sucralose can alter the gut microbiota,
the set of bacteria that live in your
intestines and play a key role in
glucose metabolism in ways that can harm
long-term blood sugar control. And
there's an important piece of data
released in January 2025, published in
the American Journal of Clinical
Nutrition by the Harvard School of
Public Health, analyzing three large
American cohorts. Coffee without any
additives was associated with a 10%
reduction in diabetes risk for each
additional cup per day. However, when
coffee was consumed with sugar, this
protective effect decreased
significantly. translated into practical
advice. Remove sugar from your coffee.
It sounds simple, but it's powerful.
Mistake number three, drinking coffee in
the late afternoon or at night. Caffeine
has a half-life of 5 to 6 hours in the
body. This means that if you drink
coffee at 3:00 p.m., half of that
caffeine is still circulating in your
body at 8:00 p.m. It keeps cortisol
levels elevated, interferes with deep
sleep, and it's during quality sleep
that the body regulates part of glucose
metabolism. Poor sleep equals
uncontrolled glucose. It's a vicious
cycle. When I explained all this to Mr.
Arnaldo and we adjusted his coffee
routine together. In 8 weeks, his
fasting glucose dropped from 138 to 112.
His HBA1C went from 7.1% to 6.6%.
Without changing medication, without a
radical diet, without going to the gym,
just by adjusting his coffee habits.
Now, I'm going to tell you how you
should drink coffee to make the most of

something. If this content is helping

right coffee at the right time. First
golden moment, breakfast, but not the
very first thing in the morning. When
you wake up, first drink a glass of
water. Wait between 30 and 60 minutes.
Eat something that contains protein.
Scrambled eggs, a piece of cheese, plain
unsweetened yogurt, any source of
protein. Only then drink your coffee and
drink it plain without sugar or
sweetener. Why protein first? Because
protein buffers the impact of caffeine
on cortisol. It provides amino acids
that the liver uses more efficiently.
And this prevents that spike in cortisol
that unnecessarily releases glucose into
the bloodstream. Research published in
the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology
showed that consuming protein before
carbohydrates and caffeine reduces the
postmeal glucose spike. And if you want
to enhance it even more, add half a
teaspoon of cinnamon powder to your
coffee. Cinnamon contains compounds that
partially mimic the action of insulin,
helping cells absorb glucose. A review
published in the journal Nutrients
showed that regular cinnamon consumption
may contribute to a modest reduction in
fasting glucose levels. It's not
medicine, but it's an ally. Second
moment, coffee before lunch. This is a
secret that few people know. About 20 to
30 minutes before your main meal, drink
a small cup of plain black coffee
without sugar. The chlorogenic acid in
coffee will act like that traffic
controller I mentioned earlier, slowing
down the absorption of sugar from the
foods you're about to eat. This reduces
the post-meal glucose spike, which is
one of the main triggers for developing
type 2 diabetes. A study conducted by
researchers at Brigham and Women's
Hospital affiliated with Harvard Medical
School and published in Diabetes Care
showed that coffee components,
especially chlorogenic acid and
trigonaline, significantly reduce
glucose and insulin concentrations in
the first 15 minutes after consuming
carbohydrates compared to a placebo.
This effect is real, measurable, and you
can take advantage of it without
spending anything. Third moment after
physical exercise. Do you walk or do any
physical activity? Then pay attention.
Within 30 minutes after finishing your
walk or any exercise, drink a cup of
plain coffee. When your muscles are
active or have just worked, they become
much more receptive to glucose. They act
like a sponge, absorbing sugar from the
blood to use as energy. Caffeine at this
moment enhances this effect, helping
muscles absorb glucose even more
efficiently. It's a metabolic window of
opportunity that most people miss. As
for the total amount of coffee per day,
the World Health Organization along with
most medical guidelines considers that
consuming up to four or five small cups
of coffee per day within a healthy
pattern is safe for most adults. The
Karolinska Institute in the review of 30
studies I mentioned found that this
amount falls within the range of
protective benefits against diabetes,
but each person is different. If you
feel palpitations, anxiety, tremors, or
insomnia from coffee, it may be that
your caffeine metabolism is slower,
which is genetically determined. In that
case, reduce it to two cups per day or
choose a quality decaffeinated option,
which also contains chlorogenic acid and
retains many of the benefits. Here's a
story I need to tell you. Donelli, 68
years old, a retired teacher, came to my
clinic with type 2 diabetes, diagnosed
three years earlier using metformin with
glucose still difficult to control. She
was the type of patient who followed
everything correctly, took her
medication, reduced rice, but her hbaw1c
stubbornly stayed above 7.5%.
When I discussed her routine, I
discovered that she drank sweetened
coffee with milk in the morning. coffee
with sweet biscuits in the afternoon and
had practically stopped moving because
her legs felt heavy. Together, we
created a simple plan. Breakfast. Wait
45 minutes after waking up, eat a boiled
egg, then have plain coffee with
cinnamon. Pre-launch coffee, a small cup
20 minutes before eating. A 25minut walk
in the afternoon three times a week and
a coffee right after. We eliminated the
sweetened afternoon coffee and the
biscuits. 6 weeks later, she returned to
the clinic with a smile I will never
forget. Her hbaw1c had dropped from 7.5%
Her fasting glucose was 109. She told
me, "Doctor, I woke up with energy for
the first time in years. I didn't change
her medication. I changed her
relationship with coffee. But now I need
to warn you about something important
that may be sabotaging you without you

enemies that destroy all the benefits
I've just described. I'll list the main
ones. Enemy number one, sugar of any
kind. Refined, brown, derara, honey.
Anything sweet added to coffee creates
an immediate glucose spike that cancels
the protective effect of chlorogenic
acid. The 2025 Harvard research made
this very clear. Coffee with sugar has
reduced protection against diabetes.
Enemy number two, artificial sweeteners.
As I explained earlier, there is growing

disrupt the balance of the gut
microbiota, which is essential for
healthy glucose metabolism. If you need

so far has not shown the same negative
effects in available research. Enemy
number three, industrialized instant
coffee, the kind that comes in sachets.
These products often contain hidden
sugar, hydrogenated fats, and additives
that compromise nutritional quality.
Prioritize natural ground coffee, 100%
Arabica, freshly brewed. Enemy number
four, coffee paired with ultrarocessed
foods, white bread, filled cookies,
industrial cakes. When you combine
coffee with these foods, the glucose
spike from refined carbohydrates is so
high that no amount of chlorogenic acid
can compensate. And finally, I'll tell
you about what I call the protection
triad because coffee works even better
when it's part of a context of healthy
habits. First element, smart coffee, the
way I taught you at the right time
without sugar, maximizing the benefits
of chlorogenic acid. Second element,
movement. You don't need to go to a gym.
30 minutes of moderate paced walking
three to five times a week is enough to
dramatically improve insulin
sensitivity. Moving muscles act like
drains, pulling glucose out of the blood
independently of insulin. This has been
confirmed by so many studies that it
would be impossible to list them all
here. Third element, adequate protein
intake in meals. Protein is the
macronutrient that has the least impact
on glucose, promotes satiety, and
protects muscle mass, which is the
body's largest consumer of glucose.
After the age of 60, protein needs
increase because the body becomes less
efficient at using it. Eggs, chicken,
fish, beans, cheese, and plain yogurt
include a source of protein in each main
meal. When these three elements work
together, the effects multiply. Coffee
improves insulin sensitivity. Exercise
increases glucose uptake by muscles, and
protein stabilizes post-meal glucose
spikes. It's a powerful combination that
doesn't cost much and doesn't require
any special product. But I need to be
honest with you about one thing.
Everything I've described here are
complimentary lifestyle strategies based
on solid scientific evidence. They can
help a lot, but they do not replace

already have diagnosed diabetes and use
medication, never change the dosage or

should take this information to your
doctor, discuss it together, and make
adjustments with professional guidance.
